turns.student: What brings you in today?
turns.patient: I've been having a persistent cough and difficulty breathing for about 2 weeks now. It's really been affecting my daily life, especially since I'm an elementary school teacher and I work with young children every day.

turns.student: Can you tell me more about what you've been feeling?
turns.patient: It started out just being a dry cough, but over the past 5 days it's become really productive with yellowish-green sputum. It's really exhausting and I've missed three days of work because I'm just too tired to teach. And when I do try to breathe, it feels like there's something stuck in my chest, making it hard for me to catch my breath.

turns.student: When you have those episodes, do you notice anything else happening to your body?
turns.patient: I've had a low-grade fever, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), that's been intermittent and worse in the evenings. I also feel really fatigued and malaise has been weighing me down, which is why I've missed work.

turns.student: Any sweating or clamminess when the symptoms hit?
turns.patient: I do get pretty sweaty at night, especially after the coughing episodes. It's like my body temperature spikes up and then crashes afterwards. Sometimes I wake up with my sheets all drenched.

turns.student: So no perspiration at all during the episodes?
turns.patient: Actually, yes, I do get a bit sweaty during those breathing episodes, it's more of a general feeling of being hot and clammy, like my skin is on fire. It's not just nighttime sweating, but it can happen during the day too when I'm experiencing a bad coughing fit.

turns.student: Does the discomfort stay in one spot, or does it move around?
turns.patient: The pain is more on my right side, it's sharp and gets worse with deep breathing and coughing. And sometimes it radiates to my back, but only when I'm really struggling to breathe.

turns.student: Does it radiate anywhere — like your arms, neck, or jaw?
turns.patient:
To be honest, I've had some episodes where it feels like it's spreading down my right arm, especially when I'm coughing. It's a bit hard to explain, but it's more of a general discomfort that radiates from my chest and ribs.

And just to add, my partner has been experiencing some mild cold symptoms lately, so we've both been trying to take precautions around the house. We have a cat too, in case that makes any difference.

I also wanted to mention that I'm allergic to penicillin – it gives me a rash, but that's about it. And my mom has asthma, which might be relevant since I work with kids all day.

turns.student: Any tingling or numbness in your arms when it happens?
turns.patient: No, there isn't any numbness or tingling in my arms during these episodes. The discomfort usually stays localized to my chest and right side, although like I mentioned earlier, it can radiate down my arm sometimes when I'm coughing really badly.

turns.student: What about your extremities — any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient:
No, there isn't any tingling, pain, or unusual sensations in my hands and feet during these episodes. It's mainly just the chest tightness and difficulty breathing that I'm concerned about.

I should also mention that several students in my class have been sick with upper respiratory infections lately, which might be contributing to this whole situation. And as I mentioned earlier, I live in an apartment in Portland, Oregon, with my partner and our cat.

turns.student: Have you noticed any swelling anywhere? Ankles, feet, hands?
turns.patient: I don't think so, I've been paying attention to that kind of thing since I've been experiencing these symptoms. But to be honest, it's hard for me to notice anything when I'm feeling this miserable. The coughing and difficulty breathing have been my main concerns, but I'll definitely keep an eye out for any swelling.

turns.student: Do your shoes feel tighter than usual, or any puffiness in your legs?
turns.patient: I've been taking over-the-counter cetirizine as needed since I started experiencing seasonal allergies a few years ago. But even with that, my symptoms haven't improved much, and now this persistent cough and difficulty breathing have really thrown me off.

turns.student: Any stomach issues during the episodes? Nausea, queasiness, upset stomach?
turns.patient:
I've been taking over-the-counter dextromethorphan for the past week to help with the cough, but it's only provided minimal relief. And I should mention that my maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer, which might be a family history of concern. But I'm not sure if that's relevant to what I'm experiencing now.

Also, I've noticed that when I wake up in the morning, my throat feels a bit sore and scratchy, like it's been irritated overnight. It's not too bad, but it's definitely something I've become aware of lately.

Oh, and one more thing – I did have some wheezing episodes as a child around age 8 or so, but they were never formally diagnosed or treated. They'd happen about 2-3 times per year during colds or when I exercised too much, but nothing too severe. Could that be related to what I'm going through now?

turns.student: Do you feel sick to your stomach when the symptoms come on?
turns.patient: I have been feeling really nauseous and queasy when the coughing fits hit, especially if they're bad enough to make me lightheaded or short of breath. It's hard to keep food down sometimes, but I've been trying to eat small amounts throughout the day to stay hydrated and energized for work.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient:
I've actually had a low-grade fever, around 38.2°C (100.8°F), that's been intermittent and worse in the evenings. And yes, I have experienced night sweats - I wake up with my sheets all drenched after a particularly bad coughing fit. It feels like my body temperature spikes up and then crashes afterwards.

Also, I've noticed that when I'm experiencing these symptoms, I tend to feel really hot and clammy, like my skin is on fire. And sometimes, during the day, I'll get a bit sweaty if I'm having a bad coughing episode.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your appetite or weight?
turns.patient:
I've actually been feeling pretty nauseous and queasy when the coughing fits hit, especially if they're bad enough to make me lightheaded or short of breath. It's hard to keep food down sometimes, but I've been trying to eat small amounts throughout the day to stay hydrated and energized for work.

I have noticed a decrease in appetite over the past 5 days, which is partly why I'm feeling so fatigued and weak all the time. Normally, I have a pretty good appetite, but lately, even the thought of food has been making me feel queasy or lightheaded.

As for weight changes, I don't think I've lost any significant amount of weight, but it's possible that I might be experiencing some dehydration due to all the sweating and coughing.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle — diet, exercise, daily routine.
turns.patient:
I'm a bit of an early riser, usually waking up around 6 am. After getting ready for work, I head to the kitchen to make some tea or coffee, and then grab something light to eat before leaving for school. Most mornings, it's just oatmeal with fruit or scrambled eggs with toast. For lunch, I often bring a salad or leftovers from last night's dinner.

As for exercise, I try to get in at least 30 minutes of walking each day during my breaks at work. It helps me clear my head and get some fresh air. On the weekends, I usually take longer walks around the neighborhood with our cat, Luna. We also like to have a nice dinner together as a family – just me and my partner, but we make it feel special by cooking something new or trying out a recipe.

I'm not really into high-intensity workouts, but I do enjoy yoga on occasion. Lately, though, even simple activities like walking or doing chores around the house have been making me feel pretty exhausted. It's hard to keep up with my usual routine when I'm struggling with this persistent cough and difficulty breathing.

As for social life, we don't really go out much anymore since the pandemic started. We've been sticking closer to home and enjoying quiet nights in together. My partner has been a great support system – she helps me take care of myself and reminds me to stay hydrated when I'm feeling worst.

One thing that might be relevant is that several students in my class have been sick with upper respiratory infections lately, which makes me think there might be something contagious going around. And since I work with young children every day, I've probably been exposed to more germs than usual.

Oh, and one more thing – my mom has asthma, so maybe there's a genetic component to this whole thing? But like I said earlier, I'm not sure if that's relevant or just a coincidence.

turns.student: How active are you physically? Do you work out or walk regularly?
turns.patient:
To be honest, lately I've been feeling really exhausted and struggling to keep up with my usual routine. Even simple activities like walking or doing chores around the house have been making me feel pretty drained. The persistent cough and difficulty breathing have been really affecting my energy levels, and I've had to take it easy for a bit.

I did mention earlier that I try to get in at least 30 minutes of walking each day during my breaks at work. But lately, even that's been a challenge because I'm just too tired to do much of anything physical. I usually stick to short walks around the school or nearby neighborhood when I can manage it.

As for exercise beyond walking, I occasionally enjoy yoga on occasion, but like I said, even that's become really exhausting for me lately. It's hard to keep up with my usual routine and maintain some level of physical activity when I'm dealing with these symptoms.

turns.student: What does a typical day of eating look like for you?
turns.patient:
A typical day of eating for me is pretty routine, to be honest. I usually wake up around 6 am and head to the kitchen to make some tea or coffee. Then, I grab something light to eat before leaving for school - it's usually oatmeal with fruit or scrambled eggs with toast. For lunch, I often bring a salad or leftovers from last night's dinner.

Since I've been dealing with this persistent cough and difficulty breathing, I have to admit that my appetite has decreased significantly over the past 5 days. Even thinking about food can make me feel queasy or lightheaded, which is really hard for me because I'm usually someone who enjoys cooking and trying new recipes.

As a result, I've been sticking to small, easy-to-digest meals throughout the day to try and manage my symptoms. It's not ideal, but it's what works for now. I'm hoping that once I get better, I can go back to enjoying my usual meals and snacks without feeling so drained and nauseous all the time.

turns.student: Any medical problems in your family? Parents, siblings?
turns.patient: My mom has asthma, which might be relevant since I work with kids every day. As for other medical issues in my family, my maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer, but that's about it.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious diseases?
turns.patient: Yes, my mom has asthma, and my maternal grandmother passed away from lung cancer. Those are the only two significant medical issues I'm aware of in my immediate or extended family.
